topic: Fundamental Tagalog Grammar Rules for Learners

rules.name: Roots and Affixes
rules.description: Roots combined with affixes form various words; affixes change meaning and grammatical role.

rules.name: Sentence Structure
rules.description: Tagalog word order differs from English; subject marking via particle markers (e.g., ang).

rules.name: Pronunciation and Sounds
rules.description: Certain vowel sounds can be interchanged in spoken Filipino.
rules.common_errors: Not explicitly stated, but note that changes occur only in spoken forms.

rules.name: Syllable Repetition
rules.description: Syllable repetition is a pattern in verb conjugation (future tense).

rules.name: Markers (ang, ng, sa)
rules.description: Markers indicate grammatical roles such as subject focus, possession, and direction.

rules.name: Gender Neutrality
rules.description: Filipino nouns and pronouns are generally gender-neutral.
examples: Siya yung sinasabi ko. (He/She was the one I was talking about.)

rules.name: Plurals
rules.description: Plural nouns use the marker "mga" before the noun.

rules.name: Ang Pronouns
rules.description: Pronouns that serve as subject focus in sentences.

rules.name: Ng Pronouns
rules.description: Pronouns for possessive or unfocused actors.

rules.name: Sa Pronouns
rules.description: Pronouns indicating location, direction, or possession.

rules.name: Kita
rules.description: Special pronoun for combinations like I–you.

rules.name: Verb Groups
rules.description: Verbs are grouped by affixes (mag-, ma-, um-, in-, i-).

rules.name: Verb Repetition for Prolonged Action
rules.description: Repeated verbs with "nang" express ongoing actions.
examples: Kain nang kain si Raul. (Raul keeps on eating.)

rules.name: Adjective-Noun Identicals
rules.description: Some adjectives have the same spelling as nouns but differ in pronunciation.

rules.name: Adjective Gender
rules.description: Certain adjectives change form based on gender (commonly end in -a for female).

rules.name: Degrees of Adjectives
rules.description: Adjectives intensify through repetition or addition of prefixes.

rules.name: Typical Word Order (V-S-O)
rules.description: Tagalog generally uses a Verb-Subject-Object order rather than English’s S-V-O, which emphasizes the action first.

examples.Formal Order: Si Maria ay nag-aaral ng Tagalog

rules.name: Declarative Sentences
rules.description: Use verb-first to state facts.
rules.common_errors: Using S-V-O order like English learners tend to do

rules.name: Interrogative Sentences
rules.description: Questions place the predicate/verb or phrase before the subject and subject particle.
rules.common_errors: Incorrect placement of ‘ba’ before the predicate

rules.name: Imperative Sentences
rules.description: Commands typically start with the verb followed by the subject.
rules.common_errors: Placing subject first like English (‘Ikaw pumasok’)

rules.name: Exclamatory Sentences
rules.description: Express emotion by leading with the adjective or predicate followed by the particle.

rules.name: Connectors for Compound Ideas
rules.description: Tagalog uses connectors such as *at* (and) and *dahil/kasi* (because) to link ideas.

examples.English: I’m tired because I worked all day

rules.name: Applying English Word Order
rules.description: English learners often incorrectly use English structures, which can make sentences sound unnatural.

examples.Correct: Gusto ko ng prutas at gulay

rules.common_errors: Using English word order or misusing connectors

rules.name: Overuse of “ay”
rules.description: Learners often overuse the particle *ay*. It’s typically for emphasis or formal contexts, not everyday speech.
rules.common_errors: Overusing ‘ay’ in casual sentences

rules.name: Quiz Corrections
rules.description: Example learner sentences with corrections to reinforce correct structure.
rules.common_errors: Direct translation from English structures

topic: Filipino Grammar for the Beginning Learner

rules.name: Active vs Non-Active Verb Affixes
rules.description: Filipino verbs are conjugated using affixes for active and non-active voices.

rules.name: Plurality in Verbs
rules.description: Verbs do not change with number in everyday Filipino.
rules.common_errors: Assuming plural must always change verb form

rules.name: Passive Voice Usage
rules.description: Passive constructions are often more natural in Filipino than direct active voice.

examples.Formal Active: Ako ay umiinom ng kape araw-araw
examples.note: More formal but uncommon in speech

rules.name: Basic Tense/Aspect Categories
rules.description: Filipino tenses are often described as aspects: completed (past), incompleted (present), contemplative (future).

examples.English: I will watch a movie tomorrow

rules.name: Imperative and Infinitive
rules.description: Imperative and infinitive forms use the same conjugation patterns but are conditioned by markers/hints in context.

rules.name: Recent Perfective Aspect
rules.description: Indicates actions completed just before speaking; applies mainly to actor-focus verbs.

rules.name: Personal Pronoun Cases
rules.description: Pronouns vary by case (nominative, possessive, objective). Some include special forms (e.g., kita).
rules.common_errors: Confusing pronoun forms by case

rules.name: Case Markers (Ang/Ng/Sa)
rules.description: Markers indicate grammatical roles such as subject focus, possession, and direction.
rules.common_errors: Misplacing markers due to English assumptions

rules.name: Verb Focus and Affix Choice
rules.description: Choosing the proper affix depends on which component (actor, object, etc.) you want to focus on.

examples.English: It was rice that I ate (object focus)

rules.common_errors: Using affixes without considering focus

topic: Filipino Grammar Rules — Quick Online Learning

rules.name: Verb–Subject–Object Order
rules.description: In Filipino, the verb typically comes before the subject and object, unlike English’s subject–verb–object order.
rules.common_errors: Forgetting the verb-first order and applying English SVO directly.

rules.name: Use of ‘ay’ for Formality
rules.description: The copula ‘ay’ is used to form more formal or emphatic sentences.

examples.Filipino: Si Juan ay maganda ang boses.

rules.common_errors: Overusing ‘ay’ in everyday informal speech.

rules.name: Personal Pronouns
rules.description: Filipino personal pronouns change depending on case (subject, object, possessive).
rules.common_errors: Confusing pronoun forms in object vs possessive use.

rules.name: Plural Pronouns
rules.description: Pronouns also indicate inclusive vs exclusive first-person plural.
rules.common_errors: Mixing up inclusive and exclusive ‘we’ forms.

rules.name: Markers ‘ang’, ‘ng’, and ‘sa’
rules.description: Filipino uses markers to show grammatical roles. ‘Ang’ marks the focus of the sentence, ‘ng’ shows possession or non-focused actors/objects, and ‘sa’ indicates direction or indirect objects.
rules.common_errors: Misplacing case markers by directly translating English noun roles.

rules.name: Plural with ‘mga’
rules.description: To make a noun plural, ‘mga’ is placed before the noun.
rules.common_errors: Forgetting to use ‘mga’ for plural nouns.

rules.name: Use of Verb Affixes
rules.description: Filipino verbs are formed with affixes that indicate tense/aspect and focus.
rules.common_errors: Using verb roots without appropriate affixes.

rules.name: Aspect over Tense
rules.description: Verb forms in Filipino indicate aspect (complete, ongoing, future) rather than strict grammatical tense.
rules.common_errors: Applying English tense rules directly.

rules.name: Adjective Placement
rules.description: Adjectives generally come before the noun they modify in Filipino.
rules.common_errors: Switching to English word order.

rules.name: Adverb Formation
rules.description: Many Filipino adverbs are formed by affixing or repeating words to express manner or degree.

rules.name: Forming Yes/No Questions
rules.description: Yes/no questions often use particles like ‘ba’ to indicate inquiry.
rules.common_errors: Omitting the question particle in direct translations.

rules.name: Negation with ‘hindi’ and ‘wala’
rules.description: ‘Hindi’ is used to negate verbs and equational sentences; ‘wala’ indicates absence.

topic: Common Filipino Language Mistakes and How to Avoid Them

grammar_mistakes.name: Incorrect Syllabication (Pronunciation)
grammar_mistakes.description: Mispronouncing Filipino words by incorrectly syllabicating them because of influence from one’s native language.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Carrying over accent and stress from native language into Filipino syllabication.

grammar_mistakes.name: Confusion between ng and nang
grammar_mistakes.description: Using “ng” and “nang” interchangeably when they have distinct grammatical roles.

examples.meaning: Jed is the owner of the car.

examples.sentence: Naliligo ako nang biglang may kumatok sa pinto.
examples.meaning: I was bathing when someone suddenly knocked on the door.

examples.sentence: Kain ka nang kain hindi ka naman tumataba.
examples.meaning: All you do is eat, yet you don’t gain weight.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Using “nang” when “ng” should be used or vice versa.

grammar_mistakes.name: Kumusta vs. Kamusta (Vocabulary)
grammar_mistakes.description: Misunderstanding the appropriateness of 'kumusta' vs. 'kamusta.'
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Using “kamusta” in formal contexts where “kumusta” is considered more correct.

grammar_mistakes.name: Barok Speak and Word Order Errors
grammar_mistakes.description: Omitting linking words or inappropriate word placement, leading to awkward or incomplete Filipino sentences.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Leaving out “si/ang/ay” and other necessary linkers in sentences.

grammar_mistakes.name: Incorrect Use of the Particle ‘ba’
grammar_mistakes.description: Placing the question particle *ba* in the wrong spot in interrogative sentences.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Placing *ba* in positions that make the sentence sound awkward.

grammar_mistakes.name: Mix-up of ikaw, ka, and mo
grammar_mistakes.description: Confusing the pronouns *ikaw*, *ka*, and *mo* and their appropriate placements.

examples.incorrect: Kumain na ikaw habang maaga pa.
examples.correct: Kumain ka na habang maaga pa.
examples.meaning: Go eat while it’s still early.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Interchanging pronouns or placing them incorrectly in sentences.

grammar_mistakes.name: Verb Conjugation with Affixes
grammar_mistakes.description: Incorrectly choosing verb affixes (e.g., using *mag-* when the verb requires *-um*).
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Using the wrong affix when forming verb tenses and aspects.

grammar_mistakes.name: Confusing Daw vs. Raw
grammar_mistakes.description: Using *daw* and *raw* without regard to the sound that precedes them.

examples.correct: Kumain daw kayo kina Andrew kagabi?
examples.meaning: They said you ate at Andrew’s last night.
They said they’re already in Manila.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Using *daw/raw* without matching them to the phonetic rule (preceding consonant/vowel).

grammar_mistakes.name: Bukod vs. Maliban (Vocabulary Usage)
grammar_mistakes.description: *Bukod* and *maliban* have different meanings (“in addition to” vs “except”) and are often interchanged incorrectly.

examples.incorrect: Maliban sa Master’s Degree, mayroon din siyang Doctor’s Degree.
examples.correct: Bukod sa Master’s Degree, mayroon din siyang Doctor’s Degree.

examples.incorrect: Lahat ng anak ni Alfonso ay nagtapos na ng kolehiyo bukod kay Mateo.
examples.correct: Lahat ng anak ni Alfonso ay nagtapos na ng kolehiyo maliban kay Mateo.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Using the wrong word to express addition vs exception.

grammar_mistakes.name: Kung vs. Kapag (Conditional vs Temporal)
grammar_mistakes.description: Confusing *kung* (“if”) and *kapag* (“when”) and their proper use depending on the sentence context.

examples.correct: Paano kung hindi ko maabutan ang tren bukas?
examples.meaning: What if I miss the train tomorrow?

examples.correct: Uuwi lang ako kapag tinawag na ako ni nanay.
examples.meaning: I’ll go home when mom calls me.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Interchanging *kung* and *kapag* regardless of cause/effect or time context.

grammar_mistakes.name: Not Practicing Enough
grammar_mistakes.description: Failing to practice Filipino regularly — the biggest mistake for language learners.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Neglecting regular speaking, listening, writing practice.

topic: 11 Common Filipino Grammar Mistakes We Use Incorrectly

grammar_mistakes.name: Kumusta vs. Kamusta
grammar_mistakes.description: Using “Kamusta” instead of the more formal/correct “Kumusta”, especially in writing or formal contexts.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Using 'Kamusta' in formal writing where 'Kumusta' is preferred.

grammar_mistakes.name: Sila vs. Sina
grammar_mistakes.description: Confusing the pronoun 'sila' (they/them) with 'sina' (used to introduce proper names).

examples.usage: Parating na sina Pedro at Juan.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Using 'sina' and 'sila' interchangeably without regard to context.

grammar_mistakes.name: Daw vs. Raw
grammar_mistakes.description: Using 'daw' and 'raw' without the phonetic rule; choice depends on the sound before the word.

examples.sentence: Dumaan daw kayo sa bahay kahapon?
Si Mika raw ang nagluto ng hapunan.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Applying daw/raw incorrectly regardless of preceding sound.

grammar_mistakes.name: Ng vs. Nang
grammar_mistakes.description: Confusing the preposition 'ng' with the conjunction 'nang'.

examples.usage: Ang may-ari ng laptop na ito ay si Jed.
examples.English: The owner of this laptop is Jed.

examples.usage: Nagluluto ako nang biglang may kumatok sa aming pinto.
examples.English: I was cooking when someone knocked on our door.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Interchanging 'ng' and 'nang' without regard to grammatical role.

grammar_mistakes.name: Bukod vs. Maliban
grammar_mistakes.description: Using 'bukod' for addition and 'maliban' for exception but often mixing them up.

examples.correct: Bukod sa pagsasayaw, marunong din siya kumanta.
examples.English: Besides dancing, she can also sing.

examples.correct: Lahat ng anak niya ay pintor maliban sa kanyang bunso.
examples.English: All his children are painters except his youngest child.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Using the wrong word to express addition vs exception.

grammar_mistakes.name: Kung vs. Kapag
grammar_mistakes.description: Confusing 'kung' (“if”) and 'kapag' (“when”) based on context.

examples.correct: Matulog ka na kapag tapos na ang iyong gawain.
examples.English: Go to bed when you’re done with your work.

examples.correct: Ano ang gagawin mo kung hindi ka makapasa ng pagsusulit?
examples.English: What will you do if you don’t pass the test?

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Interchanging 'kung' and 'kapag' without context.

grammar_mistakes.name: Use of Ikaw and Ka
grammar_mistakes.description: Incorrectly swapping 'ikaw' and 'ka'; 'ka' is used more frequently in colloquial sentences.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Replacing 'ka' with 'ikaw' without understanding usage rules.

grammar_mistakes.name: Use of Mo
grammar_mistakes.description: Misusing 'mo' as a pronoun; 'mo' can mean “your” or “you” depending on context.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Confusing 'mo' with other second-person forms like 'ka' or 'ikaw'.

grammar_mistakes.name: Pinto vs. Pintuan
grammar_mistakes.description: Using 'pintuan' instead of 'pinto' for a literal door; '-an' often indicates a place, not the object.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Incorrectly generalizing words with '-an' suffix without understanding semantics.

grammar_mistakes.name: Sa akin not Sakin
grammar_mistakes.description: Incorrectly combining 'sa' and 'akin' into one word like 'sakin'.

examples.note: two separate words or stylized as 'sa’kin'.

grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Fusing separate words incorrectly (e.g., palang, parin, nalang).

grammar_mistakes.name: Filipino vs. Tagalog Label
grammar_mistakes.description: Mistaking the terms 'Filipino' and 'Tagalog' as the same. Filipino is the national language.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Referring to the national language as Tagalog instead of Filipino.

grammar_mistakes.name: Not Practicing Enough
grammar_mistakes.description: Failing to practice Filipino regularly can be one of the biggest mistakes learners make.
grammar_mistakes.common_errors: Neglecting regular speaking and writing practice.

source: Balarila ng Wikang Pambansa (Lope K. Santos, KWF)
topic: Comprehensive Filipino Grammar (Balarila ng Wikang Pambansa)

rules.name: Filipino Alphabet (Abakada)
rules.description: The Filipino alphabet used in Balarila is the traditional Abakada with 20 letters, reflecting sound-to-letter correspondence characteristic of early Filipino orthography. (Later expanded in modern orthography.)
examples: A, B, K, D, E, G, H, I, L, M, N, NG, O, P, R, S, T, U, W, Y
rules.common_errors: Using English alphabet order and letters without regard to Filipino phonology.

rules.name: Basic Filipino Sound System
rules.description: Filipino sounds include vowels (a, e, i, o, u) and consonants, with special consideration for *ng* as a digraph representing /ŋ/.
rules.common_errors: Pronouncing *ng* incorrectly as separate letters.

rules.name: Pangngalan (Noun)
rules.description: Names of persons, places, things, and ideas; marked by case markers like *ang, ng, sa* depending on function in the sentence.
rules.common_errors: Mixing up case markers due to English assumptions.

rules.name: Panghalip (Pronoun)
rules.description: Pronouns that substitute for nouns; include personal (ako, ikaw, siya), possessive (akin, iyo), and demonstrative (ito, iyon).
rules.common_errors: Using subject pronouns in place of object pronouns incorrectly.

rules.name: Pandiwa (Verb)
rules.description: Verbs indicating action or state; marked with affixes for focus and aspect (e.g., *mag-, um-, in-*).
rules.common_errors: Incorrect affix choice (e.g., *magkain* vs *kumain*).

rules.name: Panguri (Adjective)
rules.description: Modifiers of nouns, often preceding the noun and agreeing with it in focus and emphasis.
rules.common_errors: Placing adjectives after nouns due to English influence.

rules.name: Pangawing (Linker)
rules.description: Linkers like *-ng* and *na* that connect modifiers to words.
rules.common_errors: Omitting linkers in compound expressions.

rules.name: Affixation in Verb Conjugation
rules.description: Affixes change verb focus and aspect; actor focus (e.g., *um*, *mag*), object focus (e.g., *-in*), and locative focus (e.g., *i-*).
rules.common_errors: Applying incorrect affixes across focus categories.

rules.name: Derivational Affixes
rules.description: Affixes that form nouns or adjectives from roots (e.g., *ka-*, *pag-*).
rules.common_errors: Misplacing derived forms due to English morphological assumptions.

rules.name: Basic Word Order
rules.description: Typical Filipino sentences are predicate-first (Verb-Subject-Object) though other orders are grammatically possible.
rules.common_errors: Directly applying English SVO order.

rules.name: Use of *ay* Construction
rules.description: The *ay* inversion creates emphasis or formality (*Si Juan ay guro*).
rules.common_errors: Overusing *ay* in casual speech.

grammar_sections.section_name: Mga Batayang Tuntunin sa Aspekto at Panahon (Aspect & Tense)

rules.name: Aspect over Tense
rules.description: Filipino emphasizes aspect (completed, incompleted, contemplated) rather than English-style past/present/future.
rules.common_errors: Labeling Filipino forms with strict tense terms instead of aspect.

source: Balarila ng Wikang Pambansa / KWF / DepEd K-12
topic: Filipino Verb Focus System (Pokus ng Pandiwa)

rules.name: Actor Focus with -um- infix
rules.description: The -um- infix marks the actor (doer) as the grammatical subject (ang-marked). Used for voluntary, active actions.

examples.English: The child ate a mango. (child = focus/subject)

rules.common_errors: Using mag- when -um- is required, or confusing with object focus.

rules.name: Actor Focus with mag- prefix
rules.description: The mag- prefix marks the actor as focus. Often used for activities that are more deliberate or habitual, or involve external objects.
rules.common_errors: Mixing up mag- and -um- verbs; using the wrong affix for a given root.

rules.name: Object Focus with -in suffix/infix
rules.description: The -in affix marks the object/patient as the grammatical subject (ang-marked). Highlights what is being acted upon.

examples.English: The mango was eaten by the child. (mango = focus/subject)

examples.Contrast: Kumain ang bata ng mangga. (actor focus) vs. Kinain ng bata ang mangga. (object focus)

rules.common_errors: Using actor focus when the object should be the grammatical subject; forgetting -in when the object is the focus.

rules.name: Locative Focus with -an suffix
rules.description: The -an affix marks the location, recipient, or direction as the grammatical subject. Indicates where or to whom the action is directed.

examples.Sentence: Binilhan ng nanay ang tindahan ng tinapay.
examples.English: The store was bought from by mother for bread. (store = focus/subject)

rules.common_errors: Confusing with object focus; using -in when location should be the focus.

rules.name: Benefactive Focus with i- prefix
rules.description: The i- prefix marks the beneficiary (recipient) or the thing done for someone as the grammatical subject. Also used when the object is being transferred.

examples.Sentence: Ibinigay ng guro ang libro sa estudyante.
examples.English: The book was given by the teacher to the student. (book = focus/subject, transferred object)

rules.common_errors: Confusing i- focus with actor focus; not recognizing when beneficiary should be the focus.

rules.name: Choosing the Correct Verb Focus
rules.description: The ang-marked noun phrase (grammatical subject) determines which focus affix to use. The focus matches the semantic role of the ang-marked element.

examples.Example: Kumain ang bata ng mangga.
Kinain ng bata ang mangga.
Binilhan ng nanay ang tindahan.
Ibinigay ng guro ang libro.

rules.common_errors: Mismatching the verb affix with the ang-marked noun phrase; applying English subject-verb logic to Filipino.

rules.name: Contemplated Aspect (Future) via CV Reduplication
rules.description: To form the future/contemplated aspect of -um- verbs, reduplicate the first consonant-vowel (CV) of the root word before applying the affix.
rules.common_errors: Reduplicating the wrong syllable; forgetting to reduplicate for future tense; applying English future tense 'will' without changing verb form.

rules.name: Imperfective (Ongoing) Aspect via CV Reduplication
rules.description: The ongoing/imperfective aspect adds CV reduplication to the imperfective marker. For mag- verbs: nagCCV-; for -um- verbs: CumCV-.
rules.common_errors: Using past tense form when ongoing action is intended; forgetting the reduplicated syllable.

rules.name: Full Reduplication for Intensity or Distribution
rules.description: Full word reduplication expresses intensity, distribution, repetition, or diminutive meaning. Written with a hyphen between the two parts.
rules.common_errors: Omitting the hyphen; confusing full reduplication with partial reduplication; using reduplication for wrong meaning.

rules.name: Verb Reduplication for Prolonged/Continuous Action
rules.description: Repeating a verb with 'nang' expresses that an action is done continuously or excessively.

examples.Example: Kain nang kain si Raul.
examples.English: Raul just kept eating and eating.

examples.Example: Tulog nang tulog ang bata.
examples.English: The child kept sleeping and sleeping.

examples.Example: Takbo nang takbo ang mga bata.
examples.English: The children kept running and running.

rules.common_errors: Using 'ng' instead of 'nang' in this construction; forgetting that this pattern requires 'nang'.

source: KWF Ortograpiyang Pambansa / Balarila ng Wikang Pambansa

rules.name: Choosing Between na and -ng
rules.description: Linkers connect modifiers (adjectives, adverbs, relative clauses) to the words they modify. The choice between 'na' and '-ng' depends on the final sound of the preceding word.

examples.Rule: Word ends in consonant → use 'na' (separate word)
examples.Example: maliit na bahay (small house)
examples.Note: maliit ends in 't'

examples.Example: magandang babae (beautiful woman)
examples.Note: maganda ends in 'a', so add -ng

examples.Example: malaking puno (big tree)
examples.Note: malaki + ng = malaking

examples.More Examples: mabait na bata, matalinong estudyante, masamang ugali, payak na tao

rules.common_errors: Using '-ng' after a word ending in a consonant; omitting the linker entirely; forgetting the 'n' + 'g' = 'ng' rule.

rules.name: Linker in Relative Clauses
rules.description: Linkers also connect relative clauses to the nouns they modify, following the same phonological rules.
rules.common_errors: Omitting the linker in relative clause constructions; applying English relative pronoun (who/which/that) structure.

source: Filipino Linguistics Research / DepEd K-12 / KWF
topic: Po/Opo Respect System and Speech Levels

rules.name: The Po Particle: Placement and Function
rules.description: The particle 'po' is added to sentences when speaking to elders, superiors, or people deserving respect. It is placed after the first word or phrase of the sentence (not at the very end).

examples.Note: Also shift 'ka' to 'kayo' for more respect

examples.Rule: Po is placed after the first content word of the sentence.
examples.Example: Maganda po ang panahon ngayon.

rules.common_errors: Placing 'po' at the end of the sentence instead of after the first content word; omitting 'po' when addressing elders; not shifting 'ka' to 'kayo' in formal respectful speech.

rules.name: Opo vs. Oo and Hindi po vs. Hindi
rules.description: When responding to questions, use 'opo' (respectful yes) or 'hindi po' (respectful no) when speaking to elders or superiors. Use 'oo' and 'hindi' in neutral or peer conversations.

examples.Note: Don't mix 'oo' with 'po'

rules.common_errors: Mixing 'oo' with 'po' in the same sentence; using 'oo' when 'opo' is required; forgetting 'po' in the negative response.

rules.name: Kayo vs. Ka in Respectful Speech
rules.description: When showing respect, 'ka' (informal 'you') shifts to 'kayo' (plural or respectful 'you'), and 'mo' shifts to 'ninyo' or 'nila' accordingly.

examples.Note: The shift to 'kayo/ninyo' shows respect even when speaking to a single person.

rules.common_errors: Using 'ka/mo' when 'kayo/ninyo' is required in respectful speech; forgetting the pronoun shift when adding 'po'.

source: UPCAT Filipino Review Materials / Balarila ng Wikang Pambansa
topic: Common Writing Errors: Redundancy and Incomplete Constructions

rules.name: Redundant Words and Phrases
rules.description: Redundancy (kalabisan) occurs when words that repeat an already-expressed meaning are added unnecessarily. In formal Filipino writing, especially UPCAT, redundant expressions should be avoided.
rules.common_errors: Adding direction words (pataas/pababa/pabalik) after verbs that already imply direction; doubling intensifiers.

rules.name: Sentence Fragments and Missing Elements
rules.description: Incomplete constructions occur when a sentence is missing an essential component — usually the main clause, the subject, or the predicate.

examples.Complete: Hindi siya pumasok dahil sa ulan.
Kung mag-aaral ka, magiging matagumpay ka.

examples.Note: Dependent clauses starting with 'dahil', 'kung', 'nang', 'upang', 'kahit' cannot stand alone as complete sentences.

rules.common_errors: Treating dependent clauses beginning with 'dahil', 'kung', 'nang' as complete sentences; omitting the subject or predicate in compound sentences.

rules.name: Parallelism in Coordinated Elements
rules.description: When listing actions, qualities, or things in a series connected by 'at', 'o', or similar conjunctions, all items must follow the same grammatical structure.

examples.Faulty: Mahilig siyang kumanta, sumayaw, at pagluluto.
examples.Correct: Mahilig siyang kumanta, sumayaw, at magluto.
examples.Problem: Inconsistent verb forms: kumanta/sumayaw are -um- actor focus; pagluluto is a noun
Mixing adjectives with a noun phrase

examples.Faulty: Gusto niyang magluto, pagbabasa, at matulog.
examples.Correct: Gusto niyang magluto, magbasa, at matulog.
examples.Problem: Pagbabasa is a noun, not an infinitive

examples.Rule: All items in a series must have the same part of speech and grammatical form.

rules.common_errors: Mixing nouns and verbs in a list; using different verb forms (infinitive vs. gerund vs. noun) in the same series.

topic: Hyphenation Rules for Affixed and Borrowed Words

rules.name: Hyphen with Filipino Affixes on Borrowed/English Words
rules.description: When Filipino affixes (mag-, nag-, i-, pag-) are attached to borrowed English words or words beginning with a vowel, a hyphen is used to separate the affix from the root.

examples.Note: hyphen required
hyphen after i-
hyphen required
hyphen when root begins with vowel after mag-/nag-
hyphen required
No hyphen when affix merges smoothly with root (no vowel collision)

rules.common_errors: Omitting the hyphen when affixing to vowel-starting or English words; writing 'imagtrabaho' instead of 'nag-trabaho'; incorrect spacing.

rules.name: Hyphen in Compound Words vs. Reduplication
rules.description: Hyphens are used in compound words and reduplicated forms, but NOT between a repeated verb and 'nang' in continuous action constructions.

examples.Note: No hyphen; 'nang' is a separate word
Verb repeated with nang, no hyphen

rules.common_errors: Adding hyphens to 'kain nang kain' type constructions; omitting hyphens in true compound words.

rules.name: Addition Connectors
rules.description: Words and phrases used to add information to what has already been stated.

examples.English: besides this / in addition to this
examples.Example: Matalino siya. Bukod dito, masipag din.
Dagdag pa, may kakayahan din siya sa musika.
Gayundin, kailangan nating mag-aral nang mabuti.
Dumating ang guro. Pati na rin ang mga estudyante.

rules.common_errors: Confusing 'bukod dito' (addition) with 'maliban dito' (exception); using 'gayunman' when contrast is intended.

rules.name: Contrast Connectors
rules.description: Words and phrases that introduce contrasting or opposing ideas.

examples.Example: Nag-aral siya, ngunit hindi siya pumasa.
Mahirap ang buhay. Gayunpaman, nagpatuloy siya.

examples.Connector: sa kabila nito / sa kabilang dako
examples.English: despite this / on the other hand
examples.Example: Sa kabila nito, nagpunyagi pa rin siya.
Nag-aaral ang isa. Samantala, natutulog ang isa pa.

rules.common_errors: Using 'ngunit' where a cause-effect relationship is needed; confusing contrast with concessive meaning.

rules.name: Cause and Effect Connectors
rules.description: Words and phrases that express cause-and-effect or logical consequences.

examples.Example: Nag-aral siya nang mabuti, kaya pumasa siya.
Samakatuwid, kailangan nating kumilos.
Bunga nito, nabago ang kanyang pananaw.
Hindi siya dumating dahil sa ulan.

rules.common_errors: Starting a sentence with 'Dahil' without a main clause to follow; using 'kaya' where a contrast connector is needed.

rules.name: Sequence and Summary Connectors
rules.description: Words and phrases that organize ideas in a sequence or summarize information.

examples.Sequence connectors: una (first), ikalawa (second), ikatlo (third), sa wakas / panghuli (finally), pagkatapos (after/then)

examples.Summary connectors: sa kabuuan (in summary), sa madaling salita (in short), bilang konklusyon (in conclusion), sa pangkalahatan (in general)

examples.Example (sequence): Una, basahin ang teksto. Pagkatapos, sagutin ang mga tanong. Sa wakas, suriin ang iyong mga sagot.

examples.Example (summary): Sa kabuuan, mahalagang mag-aral nang maaga para sa UPCAT.

rules.common_errors: Using 'una' as the only sequence marker without completing the sequence; overusing a single connector type in an essay.

source: UPCAT Filipino Review Materials / DepEd K-12

rules.name: Payak na Pangungusap (Simple Sentence)
rules.description: A simple sentence contains a single independent clause with a subject (paksa) and predicate (panaguri). It expresses one complete thought.
rules.common_errors: Adding unnecessary subordinating conjunctions that convert simple sentences into fragments.

rules.name: Tambalan na Pangungusap (Compound Sentence)
rules.description: A compound sentence joins two or more independent clauses using coordinating conjunctions (at, ngunit, o, kaya).

examples.Filipino: Kumain ang bata at uminom siya ng gatas.
examples.English: The child ate and drank milk.

examples.Filipino: Nag-aral siya, ngunit hindi siya pumasa.
examples.English: She studied, but she did not pass.

examples.Filipino: Pupunta ka ba, o mananatili ka na lang dito?

examples.Note: Each clause can stand alone as a complete sentence.

rules.common_errors: Using subordinating conjunctions (dahil, kung) to join independent clauses; punctuation errors (missing comma before conjunction).

rules.name: Hugnayan na Pangungusap (Complex Sentence)
rules.description: A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause introduced by a subordinating conjunction.

examples.Filipino: Kumain ang bata nang dumating ang nanay.
examples.Clause analysis: Main: Kumain ang bata; Dependent: nang dumating ang nanay (temporal)
Main: Mag-aaral ako; Dependent: kung may panahon (conditional)
Main: Natutuwa siya; Dependent: dahil pumasa siya (causal)

examples.Common subordinators: dahil (because), kung (if), nang (when - past), kapag (when - habitual), upang/para (so that), kahit (even though), habang (while)

rules.common_errors: Treating dependent clauses as complete sentences; confusing subordinating with coordinating conjunctions.

rules.name: Dependent Clause Types
rules.description: Dependent clauses cannot stand alone and must be attached to a main clause. They are introduced by specific subordinating words.

examples.Example: nang dumating siya (when she arrived)
kung umuulan (if it rains) / kapag umuulan (whenever it rains)
dahil nagtrabaho siya (because she worked)
upang matuto (in order to learn)
kahit mahirap (even though it's difficult)
kaya siya pumasa (that's why she passed)

rules.common_errors: Writing dependent clauses as standalone sentences; using the wrong type of clause for the intended meaning.

grammar_sections.section_name: Paksa at Panaguri (Subject and Predicate Analysis)

rules.name: Identifying Subject (Paksa) and Predicate (Panaguri)
rules.description: In Filipino, the predicate (panaguri) usually comes before the subject (paksa). The subject is the ang-marked noun phrase; the predicate is everything else.

examples.Note: ay-inversion: subject comes first
In ay-inversion (formal), subject appears first followed by 'ay', then predicate.

rules.common_errors: Confusing grammatical subject (ang-marked) with English subject (the doer); misidentifying subject in ay-inverted sentences.

source: UPCAT Filipino Review Materials / DepEd K-12

rules.name: Simile (Pagtutulad) and Metaphor (Pagwawangis)
rules.description: Simile explicitly compares two things using 'tulad ng', 'gaya ng', 'kasing', 'parang'. Metaphor directly states one thing IS another without using comparison words.

examples.Simile: Kasing puti ng busilak ang kanyang balat.
examples.English: Her skin is as white as cotton.

examples.Simile: Parang batong matigas ang kanyang puso.
examples.English: His heart is like a hard stone.

examples.Simile: Tulad ng agila, lumilipad ang kanyang pangarap.

examples.Metaphor: Siya ang liwanag ng aking buhay.
examples.English: She is the light of my life.
examples.Note: No comparison word — directly states identity

examples.Metaphor: Ang kabataan ay pag-asa ng bayan.
examples.English: Youth is the hope of the nation.

examples.Key difference: Simile uses comparison words; metaphor does not — it directly equates two things.

rules.common_errors: Misclassifying metaphors as similes or vice versa; not recognizing implicit comparison markers.

rules.name: Personification (Pagtatao) and Hyperbole (Pagmamalabis)
rules.description: Personification gives human qualities to non-human things. Hyperbole uses extreme exaggeration for emphasis.

examples.Personification: Sumayaw ang mga dahon sa hangin.
examples.English: The leaves danced in the wind.

examples.Personification: Kumanta ang mga ibon upang batiin ang umaga.
examples.English: The birds sang to greet the morning.

examples.Personification: Niyakap ng dilim ang buong lungsod.
examples.English: The darkness embraced the entire city.
I died waiting. (extreme exaggeration for emphasis)

examples.Hyperbole: Libo-libong beses ko na itong sinabi.
examples.English: I've said this a thousand times.

examples.Hyperbole: Maaari akong kumain ng kabayo sa gutom ko.
examples.English: I could eat a horse I'm so hungry.

rules.common_errors: Confusing personification with metaphor; not recognizing hyperbole as non-literal.

rules.name: Irony (Kabalintunaan) and Alliteration (Pag-uulit-tunog)
rules.description: Irony states the opposite of what is meant for effect or sarcasm. Alliteration repeats the same initial consonant sound in consecutive words.

examples.Irony: Napakayaman mo naman! (said to someone very poor)
Ang talino mo! (said after someone makes a mistake)
Maganda talaga ang lagay ng ating bansa! (when things are bad)

examples.Alliteration: Mapagmahal, maamo, at matapang na mandirigma

examples.Note: Alliteration is common in Filipino poetry and tongue twisters (bugtong).

rules.common_errors: Confusing irony with sarcasm (irony is a broader device); missing alliteration when consonants are not strictly at the start of words.

rules.name: Onomatopoeia (Pagsasatinig) and Apostrophe (Pagtatanong sa Wala)
rules.description: Onomatopoeia uses words that imitate sounds. Apostrophe directly addresses an absent, dead, or non-human entity.

examples.Onomatopoeia: Kaluskos ng mga dahon sa hangin.

examples.Apostrophe: O Inang Bayan, ilang taon ka nang naghihintay!
O Kamatayan, bakit mo kinuha ang aking ina?

rules.common_errors: Confusing onomatopoeia with personification; not recognizing apostrophe as a figure of speech distinct from the punctuation mark.

source: DepEd K-12 / UPCAT Filipino Review Materials
topic: Antas ng Wika (Language Registers) and Formal vs. Informal Filipino

rules.name: Pormal (Formal) Register
rules.description: Used in official settings, academic writing, government documents, news, speeches. Follows strict grammar rules, avoids colloquialisms and Taglish.

examples.Context: Academic writing, UPCAT essays, speeches, news reports

examples.Feature: Complete sentences, correct grammar, formal vocabulary
examples.Example: Ang kabataan ang pag-asa ng ating bansa.

examples.Feature: Use of full Filipino words instead of abbreviations or slang
Proper use of po/opo when appropriate

rules.common_errors: Using informal vocabulary or Taglish in formal essays; omitting required grammar markers in formal speech.

rules.name: Di-pormal (Informal) vs. Kolokyal (Colloquial) Register
rules.description: Informal register is used among friends and peers in everyday conversation. Colloquial is even more casual, with heavy use of clipped forms and slang.

examples.Formal: Magandang umaga po. Maaari po bang makahingi ng tulong?

examples.Informal: Good morning! Pwede bang humingi ng tulong?

examples.Colloquial equivalent: kain na, punta na, masaya (same, but delivery/context differs)

rules.common_errors: Using colloquial forms in formal writing; mixing registers inappropriately in academic essays.
